title: Segfault calling `MultiDict().setdefault()`
author: devdanzin
body:
Using latest git version of 3.13, calling `MultiDict().setdefault("a")` segfaults:

```python
import multidict
m = multidict.MultiDict()
m.setdefault("a")
```

The backtrace references `cpython-3.13.2`, so I tested the repro code against that version  and still got a segfault.

`multidict.__version__`
```
'6.4.3'
```

Python versions:
```
Python 3.13.3+ (heads/3.13:fdcaaad1cc7, Apr 27 2025, 10:17:23) [GCC 14.2.0] on linux
Python 3.13.2 (tags/v3.13.2:4f8bb3947cf, May  4 2025, 08:34:35) [GCC 14.2.0]
```

Found using [fusil](https://github.com/devdanzin/fusil) by @vstinner.

comments.author: webknjaz
comments.body:
I confirmed that this reproduces on pyenv's 3.13.2. It crashes with a single arg passed but works with two args:
```pycon
Python 3.13.2 (main, Feb 28 2025, 20:34:46) [GCC 14.2.1 20241221] on linux
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
>>> import multidict
>>> multidict.__version__
'6.4.4.dev0'
>>> multidict.MultiDict().setdefault('a', 'b')
'b'
>>> multidict.MultiDict().setdefault('a')
[1]    614028 segmentation fault (core dumped)  python
```

comments.author: bdraco
comments.body: Signature of the arg parser should likely only allow two args.  Is there a valid use case for one?

comments.author: webknjaz
comments.body:
This is because we mimic `dict` which has the optional default argument with a fallback to `None`: https://docs.python.org/3.4/library/stdtypes.html?highlight=dict.setdefault#dict.setdefault

> [..] *default*. *default* defaults to `None`.
